Join the Alpha Project

The Center for Genomic Experimentation & Computation seeks Research Fellows from biology, chemistry, physics, engineering, computer science, and mathematics for work on the Alpha project, a long-term multidisciplinary effort, directed by Professor Roger Brent, to model and predict the behavior of a G-protein receptor-coupled signal transduction pathways in yeast using a combined experimental and computational approach.

The core of the CGEC is the Molecular Sciences Institute (MSI) in Berkeley CA. Laboratories at four other institutions also participate in the Center. Fellows have the opportunity to work at these different sites as their research needs dictate. Research Fellow positions are available in each of the following locations in the areas listed:

  • Molecular Sciences Institute (Berkeley, CA):
    • Experimental biology - Develop ways to measure pathway output and key intermediate quantities from individual cells and populations of cells.
    • Theory and Computation - Develop computational means to simulate the behavior of individual cells and populations of cells and build quantitative models that predict cellular responses to defined perturbations.

  • Caltech (Pasadena, CA): Professor Jehoshua. Bruck (Computation and Neural Systems and Electrical Engineering)
    • Theory and Computation - Develop computational means to simulate the behavior of individual cells and populations of cells and build quantitative models that predict cellular responses to defined perturbations.
    • Computation and Design - Learn from biological systems about new principles in circuits for computation and communications and explore new design principles for artificial systems that compute, communicate, and evolve like biological cells.

  • MIT (Cambridge, MA): Drew Endy (Biological Engineering and Biology)
    • Model/Experiment Coupling - Develop and apply experimental devices and computational tools to constrain models of complex biological systems.
    • Biological Information Theory - Develop and apply theoretical frameworks for understanding the time-dependent state of molecular systems.

  • UC-Berkeley (Berkeley, CA): Professor Julia Leary (Chemistry)
    • Mass Spectrometry - Develop and apply methods to analyze protein modifications.

  • Pacific Northwest National Laboratory (Richland, WA): Richard .D. Smith (Macromolecular Structure and Dynamics)
    • Mass Spectrometry - Develop and apply methods with exquisite sensitivity for the quantitative analysis of key pathway intermediates using ultra-high-pressure RP-LC coupled with ESI-FTICR and proteomic bioinformatic tools.
Applications should be directed to the Molecular Sciences Institute:

Human Resources
The Molecular Sciences Institute
2168 Shattuck Avenue Floor 2
Berkeley, California 94704 USA

or by email: fellowships@molsci.org

Include a cover letter, CV, and 3 letters of reference. U.S. citizens from underrepresented minority groups are particularly encouraged to apply. MSI is an independent not-for-profit genomic research lab and seeks the most qualified applicants regardless of race or citizenship. MSI is an equal opportunity employer.
